subsections.title: History of IP
bullets: IP insurance is the modern name for Permanent Health Insurance (PHI) — renamed to better reflect the coverage and attract more customers.
PHI name emphasised that the insurer must pay a valid claim as long as conditions are met (eg claimant is sick and below retirement age).
Also known as disability income insurance (USA) or long-term sickness insurance.
Name change was partly to avoid confusion with PMI — PHI pays a regular income, not medical costs.

key_terms.definition: Former name for IP insurance — emphasised the insurer's obligation to continue paying valid claims.
US name for IP insurance — provides regular income during periods of incapacity.

subsections.title: Definition and Aims
bullets: IP insurance provides the insured with regular, short- or long-term payments during periods of incapacity (inability to work due to illness or injury).
Benefits are in the form of a temporary annuity — continuing until policyholder recovers, dies, or policy expires.
Aim: to replace part of income lost while unable to work due to accident or illness.
A deferred period (up to 2 years) applies before benefits become payable — reduces premium and matches customer need (State/employer usually covers short-term sickness).
Premiums do not usually increase with age (unlike PMI), but inflation-linking is common; premiums may be reviewable at portfolio level.
Policy must carefully define what constitutes incapacity — not all illness/injury covered (eg attempted suicide, unemployment, redundancy excluded).
Policy is typically long-term — multiple separate claim periods can occur without the policy ceasing.
Guaranteed premiums: cannot be changed. Reviewable premiums: can be changed at review date for the whole portfolio.

key_terms.definition: Being unable to work due to illness or injury — the insured event under IP insurance.
Period of sickness that must elapse before IP benefits are paid; integrates with employer/State benefits and reduces premium.
Premiums that can be changed at a review date — the review applies to the whole portfolio, not individual policies.
Ratio of post-claim net income to pre-claim net income. Higher ratio = lower incentive to return to work = worse morbidity experience.

exam_qa.q: Why is underwriting more difficult for IP insurance than for term insurance?
exam_qa.a: Probability of claim: IP depends on many more factors (occupation, mental health) vs term (mainly age/gender). Size of claim: IP benefit duration is unknown (depends on length of sickness), whereas term sum assured is fixed. Additional difficulties with systems, managing claims, and measuring experience.

subsections.title: The Product Cycle
bullets: IP fits into the product cycle: product design → pricing → marketing/sales → underwriting → claims management → experience monitoring → valuation.
Product design is complex — admin systems must cope with recording claim start dates, reasons, cessation dates, and causes.
Pricing must reflect practices across the product cycle (eg weaker claims management = higher premiums).
IP may be actively bought by the self-employed; others need active marketing.
Underwriting and claims management are more complex for IP than most life products — both fundamentally drive claims experience.
Experience monitoring is complicated by actuarial difficulties in measuring morbidity risk.

key_terms.definition: The iterative process: product design → pricing → marketing → underwriting → claims management → monitoring → valuation, feeding back into design.

subsections.title: General Policy Conditions
bullets: Policy conditions are the most complex part of IP design — must cover lapse, claim, recovery, partial recovery, retirement, and death scenarios.
Unclear conditions risk being interpreted by courts in favour of the claimant.
Conditions should: reflect the insurer's true intentions; give cushion against adverse events (eg war exclusions); be simple and unambiguous to aid sales, underwriting, and claims.
Simple conditions help policyholders understand coverage at sale and ensure accurate information on proposals — leading to accurate underwriting.

subsections.title: Benefit Definitions (Amount)
bullets: Replacement ratio = post-claim net income / pre-claim net income. Higher ratio → worse morbidity (lower incentive to return to work). A 1% rise may imply ~1% higher expected claim cost.
Over-insurance: benefit + other income sources must not exceed full pre-claim income — insurers typically limit benefit to 50–75% of pre-claim earnings.
Escalating benefits: benefits (and premiums) increase annually in line with inflation (eg RPI, NAEI) — protects real value of benefit; subject to maximum increase (eg 5% pa).
Proportionate/rehabilitation benefits: reduced benefit when partially incapacitated or returning to part-time work — incentivises rehabilitation.
Waiver of premium: premiums are waived during a valid claim — usually subject to a short deferred period (eg 3–6 months).
Tax treatment matters: if benefits are taxed as income, the effective replacement ratio is lower.

key_terms.definition: Situation where post-claim income (benefit + other sources) exceeds pre-claim income — creates perverse incentive not to return to work.
Reduced benefit paid when the claimant can work part-time — encourages gradual return to full employment.
Policy feature whereby premiums are not collected while a valid IP claim is in payment.
Benefit that increases each year in line with a specified inflation index, protecting its real value over long claim periods.

exam_qa.q: Why might increasing the benefit each year in line with an inflation index be popular with policyholders?
exam_qa.a: Protects the real value of benefit over time — ensures the level of cover is maintained relative to normal income as wages rise with inflation.

subsections.title: Benefit Definitions (Timings)
bullets: Waiting period: period at start of policy during which no benefits are paid — now uncommon; initial underwriting is preferred to reduce anti-selection.

Deferred period: period of sickness before benefits begin — common deferred periods: 4, 13, 26, 52 weeks (UK). Integrates with employer sick pay, reduces admin and claim costs, matches customer needs.
Split deferred: policy with two benefit levels, eg £250/week after 13 weeks rising to £500/week after 26 weeks.

Early notification clause: policyholder must notify insurer after (eg) 1 month of sickness, even if deferred period is longer — enables early rehabilitation support and thorough claim assessment before benefit starts.
Linked-claims period: deferred period is waived if sickness recurs within (eg) 26–52 weeks — encourages return to work without fear of resetting deferred period.
Expiry age/term: benefits cease at expiry age (usually retirement age — typically 50, 55, 60, or 65) or at end of fixed term. Minimum 5-year term ensures classification as long-term business.

key_terms.definition: Period at start of policy during which no benefit is paid regardless of sickness — now largely replaced by upfront underwriting.
If sickness recurs within the linked-claims period, the deferred period is waived, encouraging policyholders to attempt return to work.
Age at which IP benefits cease — typically aligned with expected retirement age (eg 60 or 65).

exam_qa.q: How does a linked-claims period encourage claimants to return to work?
exam_qa.a: Without it, a claimant who returns to work and relapses must serve another full deferred period. With a linked-claims period, they can try returning without that risk, removing a barrier to rehabilitation.

subsections.title: Claim (Incapacity) Definitions
bullets: Occupational definitions — the most common: Own occupation (most generous), Any occupation (most restrictive), Suited occupation (intermediate — unable to do work for which suited by training/experience).
'Own occupation' definition: unable to perform the specific duties of the policyholder's own job. Most generous to claimant; creates highest moral hazard.
'Any occupation' definition: unable to perform ANY occupation for remuneration. Most restrictive; rarely used in individual policies now.
'Suited occupation': unable to perform occupations for which reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. Common in group IP.
Activities of Daily Work (ADW): functional definition — unable to perform a given number of specific work-related activities. Used for higher-risk occupations.

Non-occupational definitions: Activities of Daily Living (ADL) — used for LTCI and sometimes IP for non-workers; typically 2 of 6 ADLs (washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, transferring).
Changing definitions: policy may start with own-occupation definition and switch to any/suited occupation after (eg) 2 years — reduces moral hazard for long-term claims.

key_terms.definition: Claimant is incapacitated if unable to perform the duties of their specific occupation — most generous; highest moral hazard.
Claimant is incapacitated only if unable to perform any work for remuneration — most restrictive definition.
Incapacity defined as inability to work in any occupation for which the person is reasonably suited by training, education, or experience.
Functional incapacity test: typically 2 of 6 basic tasks (washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, transferring).

exam_qa.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using the 'own occupation' definition for IP insurance?
exam_qa.a: Advantages: clear and unambiguous; policyholder knows exactly when entitled to claim; attractive product. Disadvantages: highest moral hazard — claimant may not return to work even if capable of other employment; most expensive for insurer to price; significant risk of over-insurance.

subsections.title: Risk and Rating Factors
bullets: Key rating factors: age at entry, gender (where permitted), occupation class, deferred period, benefit amount, smoker/non-smoker status, policy term/expiry age.
Occupation is a critical rating factor — affects both probability and duration of claim. Occupations are grouped into classes (eg Class 1 = professional/office, Class 4/5 = manual/hazardous).
Mental illness claims are a major risk — difficult to assess, long duration, hard to manage. Growing proportion of claims in many markets.
Geographical/regional variations in claims experience are significant — some insurers use region as a rating factor.
Claim duration is as important as claim inception rate — longer claims are far more expensive per inception.
Aggregate policy limits may be placed on the total annual benefit payable to all policyholders in a household.

key_terms.definition: Classification of occupations by claim risk — typically Class 1 (low risk: professional/office) to Class 4/5 (high risk: manual/hazardous).
Probability of a new claim starting in a given period — one of two key components of IP claim cost (the other being claim duration).

subsections.title: Variations of the Product
bullets: Short-term IP (accident & sickness, A&S): benefit limited to a maximum period (typically 1 or 2 years). Lower premium than full IP; sold widely through bancassurance and group channels.
A&S is simpler to price and administer — limited claim duration means lower tail risk.
Mortgage payment protection insurance (MPPI): pays mortgage instalments during incapacity. Often written on A&S basis.
Long-term IP (full IP): benefits potentially to retirement age. More complex underwriting and claims management.
Unit-linked IP: protection element embedded in an investment-linked contract — rare.
Group IP: employer purchases policy to cover employees. Simpler underwriting (free cover limit), different distribution, commission, and pricing basis.

key_terms.definition: Income protection with benefit capped at a maximum duration (eg 1–2 years) — lower cost and simpler than full IP.
IP product specifically designed to cover mortgage payments during a period of incapacity.
Level of group IP benefit that can be granted without individual medical underwriting — claims above this trigger individual assessment.

subsections.title: Introduction to CI Insurance
bullets: CI insurance pays a lump sum (or sometimes income) on diagnosis of one of a defined list of serious conditions, or on undergoing a specified surgical procedure.
Usually a pure protection product — sum insured payable on first insured event (diagnosis, death, or policy expiry — whichever comes first).
Regular premiums paid until the insured event occurs, the insured dies, or the term ends.
If the policyholder suffers a CI event and survives, the sum insured is paid and the policy ends.
CI insurance covers both morbidity and (sometimes) mortality risk — a death benefit may be included.
Historically grew out of the recognition that surviving a heart attack or cancer diagnosis creates a large financial need, even if not permanently disabled.

key_terms.definition: Pure protection product paying a lump sum on diagnosis of a specified serious condition or on performing a specified surgical procedure.
CI benefit structure where the CI sum insured is an advance (acceleration) of the life sum insured — if CI is claimed, the life cover is reduced.

subsections.title: Characteristics of CI Insurance
bullets: Benefit is typically a fixed lump sum — policyholder decides how to use it (pay off mortgage, fund treatment, replace income).
Unlike IP, CI does not require the policyholder to be unable to work — benefit is paid on diagnosis regardless of ability to work.
CI is usually written as long-term insurance (terms > 5 years), although short-term versions exist.
Premiums are usually level throughout the term (or reviewable), unlike IP which may escalate.
Can be sold standalone or as a rider to life insurance (accelerated or additional benefit basis).
Accelerated basis: CI sum = life sum; if CI claimed, life cover ends. Additional basis: CI sum is extra over and above the life cover.
Products may be sold on with-profits, unit-linked, or without-profits basis.
Increasing use of partial/tiered payments for less severe conditions (eg early-stage cancer) — reduces cost and moral hazard.

key_terms.definition: CI policy sold independently of any life insurance — pays solely on the occurrence of a defined critical illness.
CI benefit paid on top of (additional to) the life sum insured — both CI and death benefits can be paid.
Premiums fixed throughout the policy term — common for CI; distinguish from the escalating premiums used in some IP products.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between 'accelerated' and 'additional' CI benefit structures?
exam_qa.a: Accelerated: the CI sum is paid from (reduces) the life sum insured — if CI is claimed, life cover is extinguished or reduced. Additional: CI benefit is paid in addition to the full life sum insured, so both can be paid independently.

subsections.title: Core and Additional Conditions
bullets: Conditions covered are exhaustive (listed in the policy) — only named conditions are insured.
Core conditions (typically always included): cancer, heart attack, stroke. These three account for the majority of CI claims.

Additional conditions vary by insurer and market — may include: kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, total permanent disability, Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, blindness, deafness, loss of limbs, coma, aorta graft, coronary artery bypass, heart valve replacement.
ABI Model Wording (UK) was developed to standardise definitions — reduces disputes but limits product differentiation.
Each condition must be precisely defined — ambiguity leads to claim disputes. Definitions typically specify: diagnostic criteria, required medical evidence, and minimum severity.
Exclusions are common: pre-existing conditions, self-inflicted injuries, alcohol/drug abuse.
Survival period: typically claimant must survive a minimum period (eg 14–28 days) after diagnosis before benefit is paid — reduces the overlap between CI and death benefits.

key_terms.definition: The standard set of CI conditions always included: cancer, heart attack, stroke — account for the vast majority of claims.
UK standard definitions for CI conditions developed by the Association of British Insurers — promotes consistency and reduces claim disputes.
Minimum period the claimant must survive after the CI event before the benefit is paid (typically 14–28 days).
CI conditions that were present or symptomatic before policy inception are excluded from cover.

exam_qa.q: Why are CI conditions defined in an exhaustive list rather than a broad definition of serious illness?
exam_qa.a: Exhaustive list gives both insurer and policyholder certainty about what is covered. A broad definition creates ambiguity, claim disputes, and makes pricing very difficult. The insurer must be able to estimate the expected cost of claims for each condition separately.

subsections.title: Terminal Illness
bullets: Terminal illness (TI) benefit: an accelerated payment of the CI/life sum insured when the insured is diagnosed with a condition expected to cause death within a specified period (typically 12 months).
TI is not the same as CI — CI pays on diagnosis of specific conditions; TI pays when any condition is expected to cause death imminently.
TI benefit allows the insured to use funds while alive — for care, family provision, or bucket-list spending.
Underwriting perspective: TI accelerates what would otherwise be a death claim — limited additional risk if already covered by life insurance.

key_terms.definition: Accelerated payment of sum insured when life expectancy is less than a defined period (typically 12 months) — allows early payment while the insured is alive.

subsections.title: Total and Permanent Disability (TPD)
bullets: TPD is often included as a CI condition — pays if the insured is permanently and totally unable to work in any/own/suited occupation.
Definition of TPD is critical — must specify: occupation test (own/any/suited), permanence criteria, and medical evidence requirements.
ADL-based TPD: incapacity judged by inability to perform a specified number of ADLs — used for non-working claimants and older ages.
TPD is the hardest CI condition to assess — permanence is difficult to establish early; moral hazard is significant.
Some policies use a 'loss of limbs' or 'loss of sight' definition as a proxy for permanent disability.

key_terms.definition: CI condition where the claimant is permanently unable to work in a defined category of occupation — permanently and totally disabled.

subsections.title: Children's Benefit
bullets: Some CI policies extend cover to the policyholder's children — a reduced lump sum (eg 25% of sum insured) paid on diagnosis of a specified CI condition in a child.
Conditions for children may differ from adult conditions — certain childhood illnesses (eg Type 1 diabetes) may be included.
Often included at no extra premium — reflects relatively low probability of childhood CI claims.
Provides financial support for a parent needing to take time off work to care for a seriously ill child.

subsections.title: Definition of Tiered Benefits
bullets: Traditional CI pays full sum insured for all covered conditions — but severity of conditions varies enormously (eg early-stage basal cell carcinoma vs advanced cancer).
Tiered (or multi-benefit) CI: pays different percentages of the sum insured depending on severity of the condition.
Typically: full payment for severe conditions; partial payment (eg 10–25%) for less severe/early stage conditions.
Partial payment conditions may be payable multiple times — up to the full sum insured across all partial claims.
After a partial claim, the remaining sum insured is reduced; policy may continue for the balance.

key_terms.definition: CI design where payout varies by severity — full sum insured for serious conditions; partial payment for less severe/early-stage events.
Payment of a fraction of the CI sum insured for a less severe defined condition — policy continues for the remaining balance.

subsections.title: Reasons for Offering Tiered Benefits
bullets: Consumer need: early-stage cancers do not necessarily cause inability to work or require large financial sums — a reduced payout better matches actual financial impact.
Competitive advantage: tiered products cover more conditions and offer more perceived value.
Insurer perspective: partial payments for minor conditions are lower cost than full payment — reduces claim severity risk.
But: increases complexity — more conditions to define and administer; more potential for disputes.
Potential anti-selection: policyholders with early-stage conditions may be more likely to buy tiered products.
Pricing challenge: estimating expected partial claims requires more granular morbidity data.

exam_qa.q: What are the main problems for the insurer in offering tiered CI benefits?
exam_qa.a: Greater complexity in definitions and administration; more conditions to price (requires more data); greater risk of disputes at claim stage; potential anti-selection on tiered conditions; partial claims may be paid repeatedly, increasing overall liability.

subsections.title: Inception / Disabled Life Annuity Approach
bullets: The inception / disabled life annuity approach determines the expected cost of benefits from claims incepting in a particular year. It considers two functions: (1) the claim inception rate, and (2) the disabled life annuity.
It ignores recoveries (or makes no distinction between recoveries and death) and ignores subsequent future sickness — a simplification of the full multi-state model.

Claim inception rate (iˣᵈ): probability that a claim will become payable to an individual aged x to x+1. The individual became sick d weeks/months earlier and remained sick through to benefit commencement.
Sickness inception rates ≠ Claim inception rates. Sickness inception = point individual first unable to work. Claim inception = end of deferred period when benefit payments commence.
Claim inception rates are derived from sickness inception rates by multiplying the sickness inception rate by the probability of remaining sick throughout the deferred period.

Disabled life annuity: present value at the date of claim inception of expected claim payments until policy expiry, allowing for any escalation of the claim amount, interest, and the probabilities of death and recovery.

Expected PV of claims incepting in a particular year = (1) survival probability (eligible to claim) × (2) claim inception rate × (3) disabled life annuity value × (4) discount factor × (5) annual benefit amount.
The survival probability excludes policyholders already claiming and those who have died before the year under consideration.

key_terms.definition: Probability that a claim becomes payable in a given year of age, after surviving the deferred period. Not the same as sickness inception rate.
PV at claim inception of expected benefit payments until policy expiry, allowing for escalation, interest, death and recovery.
Probability that policyholder is alive, not already claiming, and eligible to claim in the year under consideration.

exam_qa.q: When are sickness and claim inception rates equal?
exam_qa.a: When there is no deferred period, so benefit payments commence immediately as the policyholder falls sick.

subsections.title: Multi-State Modelling
bullets: Multi-state modelling is the more complex alternative to the inception/annuity approach. Policyholders are separately tracked through: (1) healthy premium payers, (2) lives falling sick within deferred period, (3) lives becoming claimants after deferred period, (4) lives recovering and reverting to premium payers, (5) lives dying.
Each subclass has its own set of transition probabilities: sickness inception, lapse, mortality, recovery, policy expiry.
Probabilities may vary by number of previous claims and by duration within a given state — making the model potentially very complex with hundreds of sub-cohorts.
In practice, lack of detailed credible data typically requires combining sub-cohorts and/or reducing the number of transition intensities required.
Transition intensities must be converted into transition probabilities before constructing projections of numbers in each state at future ages.

key_terms.definition: The instantaneous rate of moving from one state to another (e.g. healthy to sick). Must be converted to transition probabilities for use in projections.
A group of policyholders sharing a common history of states (e.g. same number of prior claims, same duration in current state).

subsections.title: CI Incidence Rates
bullets: CI pricing is much simpler than IP because the benefit is a lump sum rather than an income.
Overall CI incidence rate: iˣ = iˣʰᵈ + iˣˢ + iˣᶜ + iˣᵒ, where hd = heart disease, s = stroke, c = cancers, o = other causes.

Overlaps: a person may suffer both cancer and heart disease, but only one claim is paid. If claim experience data is used to derive incidence rates, double-counting is automatically avoided by attributing each claim to a single main cause.

Double-counting risk arises when population medical records (not insurance claims data) are used, as they may record disease incidence by cause without indicating whether other diseases are present simultaneously.

key_terms.definition: Rate at which policyholders are diagnosed with (and claim for) a covered critical illness in a given year of age. Sum of cause-specific rates.
Overstating combined CI incidence by counting a single claimant under multiple cause-specific rates. Leads to overstatement of claim cost (standalone) or understatement (accelerated).

subsections.title: Accelerated CI Insurance
bullets: Accelerated CI claim incidence rate: iˣ = iˣ + (1 − kˣ) × qˣ, where iˣ = CI incidence rate, kˣ = proportion of deaths due to CI, qˣ = mortality rate.
The term (1 − kˣ) × qˣ adjusts the mortality rate to remove deaths already counted as CI claims; equivalently, kˣ × qˣ reduces CI incidence to remove those CI events already captured as deaths.
Double-counting risk (overstating kˣ): understates claim cost → inadequate premiums and reserves — a solvency risk. This is the more serious error for accelerated CI.
Expected claim cost = claim incidence rate × CI sum insured.

key_terms.definition: Proportion of deaths in year of age [x, x+1] that are due to critical illness. Used in accelerated CI incidence rate formula.
CI benefit paid on earlier of death or CI diagnosis. The CI sum insured is an advance of the death benefit; death benefit is reduced by any CI payment made.

exam_qa.q: How does double-counting of kˣ cause problems for accelerated CI?
exam_qa.a: Overstating kˣ understates the net claim incidence rate, which understates the cost of claims, leading to inadequate premiums and reserves — a solvency risk.

subsections.title: Standalone CI Insurance
bullets: Standalone CI claim incidence rate = iˣ × probability of surviving the survival period.
Only claimants who survive the defined survival period receive the benefit, so the raw CI incidence rate is multiplied by this survival probability.
Double-counting for standalone CI leads to overstatement of incidence rates → uncompetitive premiums (less serious than understating for accelerated CI, as it does not threaten solvency).
The formula is applied to each CI condition separately and then combined to produce the overall risk premium.

key_terms.definition: Minimum period the claimant must survive after CI diagnosis before the benefit is paid (typically 14–28 days). Reduces overlap between CI and death benefits.
CI policy that pays only on diagnosis of an insured condition; no death benefit. Priced purely on CI incidence × survival probability.

exam_qa.q: Why is double-counting less serious for standalone than accelerated CI?
exam_qa.a: For standalone CI, double-counting overstates incidence rates, leading to uncompetitive premiums but not threatening solvency. For accelerated CI, double-counting understates rates, which can lead to inadequate reserves and premiums — a solvency risk.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: LTCI pricing is very similar to IP: either inception / annuity methodology or multi-state modelling can be used.

Multi-state model tracks transitions between dependency levels, e.g.: State 0 (Healthy) → State 1 (Benefit level 1) → State 2 (Benefit level 2) → State 3 (Benefit level 3) → State 4 (Dead). Transitions to lower benefit levels may be assumed impossible.
Forces of transition between all states are required for each arrow in the state diagram. Transition rates may need to vary by duration in current state and by cause of incapacity.

Using inception/annuity for LTCI: three claim inception rates are needed at each future age (one per benefit level). Each is multiplied by the corresponding disability annuity value and the incremental benefit at that level.

Incremental benefit pricing: if Level 1 = £20/day, Level 2 = £50/day, Level 3 = £150/day, then the additional benefit at Level 2 is £30/day (not £50), because Level 1 benefit continues into all higher levels.
Expected PV of benefits = Σₜ [p(survive to t+½) × vᵗ⁺½ × (inception rate level 1 × annuity level 1 × benefit 1 + inception rate level 2 × annuity level 2 × incremental benefit 2 + ...)].

key_terms.definition: The additional daily/weekly benefit payable on transition to a higher care level, not the total benefit at that level. Used in the inception/annuity approach to avoid double-counting lower-level benefits.

exam_qa.q: How can advances in medical science affect LTCI modelling?
exam_qa.a: They can: (1) reduce transition rates to higher benefit levels (better treatments allow people to stay independent longer); (2) increase mortality improvement (prolonging life and thus care duration, increasing claim costs); (3) alter care costs (drugs, mobility aids etc).

exam_qa.q: What inflation index would you use for LTCI care costs?
exam_qa.a: Ideally a long-term care cost index (e.g. residential/nursing home fees index). Failing that, a national earnings index is most suitable as LTC is a people industry. A blend weighted toward earnings with some weight on price inflation would typically be used. Additional inflationary factors specific to LTC must also be considered.

subsections.title: Calculating the Risk Premium
bullets: PMI is a short-term product priced on an incidence × average claim cost basis.

Risk premium (age, gender) = Σₖ [iₖ × ACₖ], where iₖ = claim incidence rate for benefit/procedure class k, and ACₖ = expected average claim cost for class k. Summation is over all benefit/procedure classes k.
iₖ and ACₖ are specific to each age, gender, and product level (e.g. hospital band). Age bands (5- or 10-year) may be used to ensure sufficient data in each cell.

Rating factors beyond age and gender — location, hospital band, smoker status, occupation — may be used depending on market practice, legislation, and competitive pressure. What competitors do is often the strongest driver toward more risk classification factors.
Equality legislation in some territories prevents or restricts differential pricing even by age and/or gender.
Multi-state modelling is used if premiums are guaranteed for longer than one year (similar approach to long-term products).

Data collection process: (1) Choose base period; (2) Collect and check data; (3) Split into homogeneous groups; (4) Analyse and identify trends; (5) Adjust and project forward to obtain future risk premiums.

key_terms.definition: Element of premium covering expected claims only. No allowance for expenses, profit etc. Calculated as Σ [incidence rate × average claim cost] across all benefit/procedure classes.
Category of treatment or procedure (e.g. surgical, diagnostic, physiotherapy) for which separate incidence rates and average claim costs are estimated.

subsections.title: Adjustments to the Risk Premium
bullets: Excess: reduces both incidence (only claims above the excess are paid) and average claim cost (claim amounts are reduced by the excess). Three adjustments needed: (1) reduce incidence for proportion of claims below the excess; (2) reduce incidence further for disincentive effect (especially voluntary excess); (3) reduce average claim cost for the excess (not by the full excess amount, as some claims are below it). Claim amount distribution may need to be assumed.

No Claims Discount (NCD): policyholders with consecutive claim-free years receive a premium discount. A claim reduces or eliminates the discount. NCD acts like an excess by discouraging small claims. Multi-state NCD model required to capture policyholder movement between NCD states.

Gender-neutral pricing: if required, weight gender-specific risk premiums by expected male:female ratio among new policyholders. Risk: if one gender is materially cheaper, anti-selection results — disproportionate uptake by the cheaper gender worsens actual claims experience vs assumptions.
Coinsurance and other product features that impact incidence or average cost must also be reflected.

key_terms.definition: Premium reduction for policyholders with consecutive claim-free years. A claim (or multiple claims) reduces or eliminates the discount. Discourages small claims similarly to an excess.
Model tracking policyholders across multiple NCD states (e.g. 0%, 10%, 25%, 40% discount levels), with transition probabilities based on claim experience.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how you might allow for an excess in calculating the PMI risk premium.
exam_qa.a: Three adjustments: (1) Reduce incidence to allow only for claims expected to exceed the excess — may require assuming a claim amount distribution; (2) Reduce incidence further for the disincentive effect of the excess (especially if voluntary); (3) Reduce average claim cost to allow for the excess — the reduction is less than the full excess amount as some claims would have been below it.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: Accident and sickness (ASU) insurance is short-term IP (typically providing benefits for up to 5 years), often used to protect loan repayments.
Rating method follows the inception / annuity approach, as with standard IP.

Average rate approach: an average rate per £ of loan is often agreed for a particular lender or scheme, charging the same premium to all policyholders regardless of age or gender. This simplifies administration but introduces cross-subsidy across the age/gender mix.
Large ASU schemes are rated using group techniques (experience rating), covered in Chapter 18.

key_terms.definition: Single premium rate per £ of loan applicable to all policyholders in a lender/scheme, regardless of individual risk characteristics. Involves cross-subsidy between age/gender groups.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: Premiums determined by calculating expected claims for each benefit separately: expected claims per benefit = inception rate × average benefit expected to be paid (adjusted for any excess or coinsurance).
Benefits covered typically include: dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, daily hospital cash, nursing care, hearing aids, specialist consultations. Each benefit will have annual payment limits.
For indemnity-type benefits within cash plans (e.g. 50% of physiotherapy costs up to £200/year), expected average cost of the benefit is used rather than the defined benefit amount.
Coinsurance with the policyholder is common to reduce anti-selection and keep premiums affordable. An excess may also apply (e.g. benefit paid only from 4th consecutive day of hospital stay).
Flat-rate pricing over broad age ranges is common despite age-varying incidence — approximations are made based on prior claim experience.

Adjustment for inertia: people's propensity not to claim even when entitled. Two causes: (1) policyholders buy for one specific benefit and forget others; (2) benefit amounts are small in absolute terms, combined with customer apathy. No inertia adjustment needed if already embedded in the company's own claims experience data.
Inertia level may change if marketing strategy changes or if market awareness of cash plan benefits increases — monitor and update accordingly.

key_terms.definition: The propensity of policyholders not to claim even when entitled, due to small benefit sizes and low awareness of all covered benefits. Reduces actual claim frequency below theoretical maximum.

subsections.title: IP Insurance — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Transition intensities: must separate out first, second, and subsequent inceptions — prior claimants are more likely to claim again.
Recovery rates must vary by duration of claim (recovery rates reduce as duration increases) and by cause of incapacity.

Sales volumes are particularly hard to estimate: depend on economic and political factors; IP markets are typically under-insured; competitive position can dramatically affect sales; claims mishandling can damage reputation.

Transition intensities (and sales volumes) are influenced by: economic morale (downturn encourages prolonged claims; self-employed less motivated to return to work), government welfare provision (high State benefits reduce IP demand and reduce return-to-work incentive), and tax (premium taxes reduce sales; tax relief increases sales).

Modelling complications: multi-state movement hierarchy; guaranteed vs reviewable alternatives (guarantees require full allowance and suitable risk margins; reviewable products allow modelling only to next review date but competitive pressures and anti-selective lapses must still be considered).

Data limitations: published IP claim inception tables have limited credibility; worldwide statistics (especially from USA) are plentiful but may lack relevance due to heterogeneity in target markets, underwriting strategies, policy wordings, and claims management.

exam_qa.q: What is the implication of limited credibility of published IP parameters for modelling?
exam_qa.a: Cannot rely heavily on standard tables. Must seek alternative data sources: reinsurers, population statistics, own data if sufficient. If data is universally insufficient: (1) assume large margins for risk; (2) issue only reviewable products; (3) ensure product is well protected by reinsurance.

subsections.title: CI Insurance — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Must estimate up to 40 different condition distributions separately, including future trends for each — practical data is unlikely to support this for all but the most common conditions.
Medical advances affect new and in-force business differently: conditions can be removed from cover on new policies; existing guaranteed policies cannot be changed (unless reviewable).
Earlier diagnosis increases claim incidence rates — condition is diagnosed before severe deterioration, meaning more people qualify for the benefit.
Simpler/more available operations: may increase treatment-based CI claims.

Modelling should use separate approaches for disease-based vs treatment-based claims, as future trends differ — medical advances may reduce disease claims (prevention) but increase treatment-based claims (more available procedures).

Guaranteed vs reviewable: as for IP, guaranteed products require full risk margins; reviewable products allow modelling to next review but must still allow for competitive pressures and anti-selective lapses.
Data limitations: CI has not been around long enough for credible claims experience except possibly for cancers and heart attacks.

exam_qa.q: Why should disease-based and treatment-based CI claims be modelled separately?
exam_qa.a: They are subject to different future trends. Medical advances may prevent diseases (reducing disease-type claims) while simultaneously making treatments more available (increasing treatment-based claims). Modelling them together would obscure these diverging trends.

subsections.title: LTCI — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Claim frequency: limited experience data on transition rates — a major difficulty in most territories.

Claim amount: where policy pays funding-for-care (indemnity) benefits, must estimate a probability distribution of annual/monthly claim payments. Claim amounts depend on economy, inflation, and capacity of care provision (demand likely to grow faster than supply, driving real cost increases above general inflation).
Portfolio projection demand is very dependent on political commitment: government policy on State LTC funding directly affects new business volumes and mix.
Data limitations: absence of insurance statistics — very little pooled industry data for LTCI.

subsections.title: PMI — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Claim frequency: difficult to define a discrete claim (one event may involve multiple payments across a chain of care). Must establish a consistent claim definition used for both frequency and amount estimation.

Claim frequency parameters depend on: duration from inception (longer-duration policyholders more likely to claim as new conditions arise beyond the pre-existing exclusion period), age, gender, time of year, occupation.

Claim amount: indemnity, often without a ceiling. Distribution depends on hospital capacity (reduced supply increases costs), medical science progress (may increase or decrease costs), insurer/provider deals (reduce costs and variance), inflation (increases costs).
Macroeconomy: affordability pressure reduces demand; near recession onset, group members may accelerate treatment while still covered.
Style of government: affects State provision attractiveness and whether tax incentives exist for PMI.
Level of market competition: more competition → lower premiums; product differentiation makes direct comparison harder.
NCD multi-states: must model policyholder movement between NCD levels.
Reclaim of initial costs: renewal assumptions should be conservative (low) to reduce risk of unrecouped initial expenses if lapses are higher than assumed.

Claim chain: PMI claims often consist of multiple payments (operation + follow-up physiotherapy etc) — the full chain of claim events must be modelled. Settlement delay is usually short for PMI but must be allowed for.
Data limitations: absence of pooled industry statistics in many markets; family cover and group arrangements mean individual risk details may not be known.

exam_qa.q: Why is estimating PMI claim frequency difficult?
exam_qa.a: The difficulty lies in defining what constitutes a discrete claim. A single treatment event may involve multiple separate payments. A consistent claim definition must be established and used for both frequency estimation and claim amount modelling.

exam_qa.q: Why does PMI claim frequency increase with policy duration?
exam_qa.a: Pre-existing conditions are typically excluded at outset (via underwriting or moratorium). As the policy continues, new medical conditions arise and are covered. Longer-duration policyholders have also self-selected for continued cover, suggesting higher expected future claims.

subsections.title: Other Modelling Issues (All Products)
bullets: Role of genetics: genetic tests can reveal predispositions to certain conditions. In some countries (e.g. UK) this information does not have to be disclosed at underwriting, creating potential for increased anti-selection. Extent of claims impact from this practice is still emerging.
Trends in anti-selection: people who consider themselves high risk are most likely to apply for cover. Underwriting is the primary tool to combat this.
Quality of underwriting: good underwriting produces more optimistic pricing assumptions (e.g. lower CI rates) than ununderwritten business. Must be consistently reflected in pricing assumptions.

key_terms.definition: Individuals with known genetic predispositions to certain conditions may preferentially seek cover without disclosing this information, worsening the insured risk pool relative to pricing assumptions.

subsections.title: Key Differences from Individual Business
bullets: Free cover limit: group schemes may have a free cover limit below which members are not individually underwritten, reducing anti-selection risk.

Control of the intermediary: an intermediary may influence available data; the insurer must ensure information is accurate and consistent with competitors. The insurer may also want to influence group policyholders to reduce work-related health risks and encourage return to work, but this may be hindered by the intermediary.

Limited insured information: full details of individual members may not be known at the start of the insurance period. Changes in staff also occur during cover. A deposit premium is paid upfront, followed by a retrospective adjustment premium at period end.

Changes in the workforce can significantly alter the riskiness of a group: leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on reason for leaving; joiners are typically healthy (especially if young); downsizing may prompt members to claim while still covered; workforce expansion from a merger/acquisition requires careful review.
Flexible benefits (cafeteria plans) increase anti-selection risk by permitting individuals to choose covers and amounts. The insurer may impose limits and restrictions on benefit choices.

key_terms.definition: The level of benefit below which group members are automatically accepted without individual underwriting.
An upfront premium paid at the start of the group insurance period, subject to retrospective adjustment at period end based on actual membership.
A scheme structure allowing employees to choose from a menu of benefit options, increasing anti-selection risk for the insurer.

exam_qa.q: Why do changes in the workforce affect claims experience under a group scheme?
exam_qa.a: Leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on their reason for leaving. Joiners tend to be young and healthy. Downsizing may encourage remaining members to claim while still covered. Merger-driven growth may improve experience initially but needs careful monitoring.

exam_qa.q: Why does the insurer need control over the intermediary in group business?
exam_qa.a: The intermediary influences the data available to the insurer and may prevent the insurer from directly encouraging policyholders to reduce health risks or return to work, both of which affect claims experience.

subsections.title: Standard Rating Approach
bullets: The usual procedures apply: judge data for relevance to future experience; subdivide into homogeneous risk cells; adjust to make appropriate for future experience (both claim incidence and claim size, e.g. for inflation).
Rating factors for group business include individual-level factors (age, gender, occupation) and group-level factors (industry, location).
The book rate is the insurer's standard premium for a group of given characteristics, before any adjustment for that group's own past experience.

key_terms.definition: The insurer's standard premium rate for a group based on general rating factors, before experience rating adjustment.

subsections.title: The Credibility Formula
bullets: The renewal premium under experience rating is: RP = Z × A + (1 − Z) × E + L, where E is the book premium, A is the risk premium based on the group's own past experience, Z is the credibility factor (0 ≤ Z ≤ 1), and L is an expense/profit loading.
The credibility factor Z depends on the volume and reliability of the group's past experience data. Higher Z means more weight on the group's own experience.
Premiums for which Z > 0 are experience rated.

Experience rating is used because a book rate may be inappropriate due to peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or the employer's attitude to safety. The group's own claims history is often the best predictor of future claims.
Experience rating makes premiums more competitive and reduces the need for a large number of rating factors. It is unsuitable for new or very small groups with insufficient past data.

key_terms.definition: A system where the premium for a group contract depends wholly or partly on the group's own past claims experience.
The weight given to the group's own experience in the premium formula, ranging from 0 (full book rate) to 1 (full experience rate).
The estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming period, calculated from past experience adjusted for changes in membership, cover and medical inflation.

exam_qa.q: Why is experience rating used for most group health contracts?
exam_qa.a: A single book rate may be inappropriate due to the peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or employer safety attitudes. The group's own past claims history, suitably adjusted, is often the best predictor of future claims. It also makes premiums more competitive and reduces the number of rating factors needed.

exam_qa.q: When is experience rating unsuitable?
exam_qa.a: When the group's past claims experience is insufficient or unreliable, for example for a new or very small group.

subsections.title: Burning Cost Method
bullets: The burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the coming period, calculated from past years' experience adjusted for: changes in the numbers insured, changes in the nature of cover, and medical inflation.
Yearly burning cost calculations compare emerging claim costs against risk premiums paid, providing the earliest possible feedback on premium adequacy.
Large individual claims are typically stripped out of the base data and added back using a grossing-up factor based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies.
Claims data must be adjusted to a full year (e.g. if only 9 months of data are available for the most recent year) and inflated to the midpoint of the prospective cover period.
Trend analysis is performed on the per-employee cost to identify and project any underlying deterioration or improvement.

exam_qa.q: How are large claims handled in a burning cost calculation?
exam_qa.a: Large claims are stripped out of the base claims data. A grossing-up factor (based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies) is then applied to add back an allowance for large claims, smoothing the distortion caused by one-off large events.

subsections.title: Group PMI Pricing Features
bullets: Experience rating is used extensively for group PMI pricing, because group PMI experience differs significantly between employers even in the same industry.

Reasons experience varies between groups: different geographical locations affect private medical treatment costs; nature of work affects employees' medical needs; employer attitudes to health and safety differ.
NCD systems may be used alongside experience rating to incentivise good claims behaviour.
Larger groups are often flat rated according to benefit class (e.g. all members of the same plan tier pay the same rate). The largest groups may self-insure.
Experience rating for group PMI encourages employers to actively manage claims and promote workplace health, reducing overall costs.
Applying group-specific premiums improves marketability and sales volume by making pricing fairer for low-claims groups.

key_terms.definition: Charging the same premium rate to all members within a benefit class, typically used for larger group PMI schemes.
Where the largest employers bear claims risk themselves rather than transferring it to an insurer, often with reinsurance above a certain threshold.

exam_qa.q: Why is group PMI experience rated rather than book rated?
exam_qa.a: Group PMI experience varies significantly between employers even in the same industry, due to differences in location (affecting treatment costs), work practices (affecting health needs), and employer attitudes to health and safety. Past claims history is a powerful predictor of future experience, and experience rating encourages employers to reduce claims costs.

subsections.title: Overview of Profit Criteria
bullets: A profit criterion is a single figure that tries to summarise the profit signature of a contract. The profit signature is the sequence of profits from inception to termination — it is the definitive guide to profitability but unwieldy for comparison.
Three main profit criteria: Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and Discounted Payback Period (DPP).
Sensitivity tests should be performed throughout the pricing process to ensure different future scenarios do not lead to serious financial difficulty.

key_terms.definition: The sequence of expected annual profits from policy inception to termination, used as the definitive measure of a product's profitability.
A single figure derived from the profit signature (e.g. NPV, IRR, DPP) used to compare the profitability of different product designs.

subsections.title: Net Present Value (NPV)
bullets: NPV is calculated by discounting the profit signature at the risk discount rate. A positive NPV means the rate of return exceeds the risk discount rate target.
Economic theory states NPV is the best profit criterion: an investor should choose the investment with the higher NPV to maximise company value.
NPV is subject to the law of diminishing returns — a company cannot sell unlimited policies with positive NPV as sales costs rise as the market saturates.
NPV is typically expressed as a percentage of initial commission (reflecting sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (reflecting market share).
NPV says nothing about competition — there is no point designing a high-NPV contract that cannot be sold.

key_terms.definition: The discounted value of the profit signature at the risk discount rate. The theoretically optimal profit criterion.

exam_qa.q: How is NPV expressed to make it useful for comparing products?
exam_qa.a: Either as a percentage of initial commission (measuring profit relative to sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (measuring profit relative to market share). A positive NPV indicates the product meets the risk discount rate target.

exam_qa.q: What are the drawbacks of NPV as a profit criterion?
exam_qa.a: It is subject to the law of diminishing returns; it says nothing about competition; comparisons are only valid if capital markets are perfectly free and efficient and the risk discount rate correctly reflects the riskiness of each investment.

subsections.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) and Discounted Payback Period (DPP)
bullets: IRR is the rate at which the discounted value of cashflows is zero. A higher IRR is generally preferred, but IRR does not always agree with NPV.

Disadvantages of IRR vs NPV: may not be unique if the profit signature changes sign more than once; cannot be related to sales effort or market share; may not exist if profits are made from the outset.
IRR is easier to understand conceptually but is not more informative than NPV in most cases.
DPP is the duration at which cumulative discounted profits equal zero — the time to recover the initial capital investment at the risk discount rate.
DPP ignores all cashflows after the payback period, so will not agree with NPV. It is useful for capital-constrained companies.
Common approach: use NPV (expressed as % of initial sales costs) as the primary criterion, with DPP as a secondary reference. DPP is more useful for product design than pricing.

key_terms.definition: The discount rate at which the NPV of the profit signature equals zero. Higher IRR is preferred, but has disadvantages vs NPV.
The policy duration at which the accumulated discounted profits equal zero. Useful for capital-constrained companies but ignores later cashflows.

exam_qa.q: Why might IRR not be a unique value?
exam_qa.a: If the profit signature changes sign more than once (i.e. profits alternate between positive and negative), there may be multiple discount rates at which NPV equals zero, so IRR is not unique.

subsections.title: Marketability
bullets: Premiums produced by the pricing model must be assessed for marketability. This may lead to reconsideration of: the product design; the distribution channel; the company's profit requirement; whether to proceed at all.
Re-examination of assumed expenses is also required, notably acquisition costs (commission), marginal administration costs, and contribution to fixed overheads.

subsections.title: Competitiveness
bullets: A close watch must be kept on competitors' pricing. Significant discrepancies should be investigated, though differences in target markets may explain them.
If a competitor uses more detailed risk differentiation, better risks migrate to the competitor — the insurer’s averaged pricing assumptions are called into question (anti-selection).
Two factors: (1) overall premium level vs competitors; (2) number and accuracy of rating factors used.
Fixed expenses are spread over volume — lower-than-expected volume increases per-policy expense loadings and reduces competitiveness (economy of scale principle).
Loss leaders: products priced below the full premium to gain market share, compensated by higher margins on other lines. Risk: adverse business mix if loss-leader sells more than high-margin lines.
Over time, each product should be brought back in line to produce its fair contribution to profits.
Return on capital: each profit centre must meet its return on capital target. Excess return suggests pricing can be relaxed to promote volume; insufficient return is grounds for withdrawal.

key_terms.definition: A product priced below its full expected cost to gain market share, with the shortfall compensated by margins on other product lines.
Any collection of business items with a separate target for contribution to insurer profit, ranging from a broad business line to a very specific segment.

exam_qa.q: What are the two factors involved in the competitive impact on pricing?
exam_qa.a: (1) The overall level of premiums compared with competitors; (2) the number and accuracy of rating factors used. Using fewer rating factors than competitors creates anti-selection as better risks go elsewhere.

sections.title: Reserving, Solvency Capital and Reinsurance Impact

subsections.title: Cost of Reserving and Solvency Capital
bullets: Writing a policy creates a need for regulatory reserves and solvency capital. Each policy should be charged for the cost of this capital.

The charge is the investment cost of holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely. Simplistically: if IRR is 10% and statutory assets yield 4%, the cost is 6% p.a. of the solvency capital requirement.

Reserving assumptions in the pricing model should match those the insurer realistically expects to use (prudent if regulations require prudent reserves; best estimate if regulations require best estimate reserves).
Solvency capital requirements may be reduced by suitable reinsurance — reinsurers may hold less capital per unit of risk, with cost savings potentially passed to consumers.

key_terms.definition: The charge in pricing for the investment return foregone by holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely.

exam_qa.q: Should reserving assumptions in the pricing model be best estimate or prudent?
exam_qa.a: They should match the assumptions the insurer realistically expects to use in practice under regulation — prudent if the regulatory regime requires prudent reserves, best estimate if the regime requires best estimate reserves. The pricing model must also allow for any solvency capital requirements on top of reserves.

subsections.title: Reinsurance Impact on Pricing
bullets: Reinsurance roles in pricing: technical assistance/data for new products; risk sharing; smoothing profitability; financing new business strain; tax arbitrage; solvency capital arbitrage; enabling acceptance of larger risks.
Unless arbitrage is possible, the insurer will expect a net loss from reinsurance (premiums paid > expected recoveries), which must be included in the pricing model.
Where financial benefits of reinsurance exceed costs (e.g. tax arbitrage), pricing net of reinsurance can improve competitiveness.
Under quota share arrangements, the reinsurer may require the right to vary commission if the insurer changes its premium rates.

exam_qa.q: Why does reinsurance usually have a net cost to the insurer?
exam_qa.a: The reinsurer must cover its own expenses and profit, so reinsurance premiums will generally exceed the expected present value of reinsurance benefits. This net cost must be included in the pricing model unless some arbitrage (e.g. tax or capital) makes the arrangement financially beneficial.

subsections.title: Regulatory Impact on Pricing
bullets: Pricing basis may be prescribed by the regulator (e.g. community rating) or certain rating factors restricted (e.g. gender, genetic test results).
Regulatory restrictions on operations (e.g. investment constraints, requirement to collect premium taxes) impose costs that must be reflected in pricing assumptions.
Such additions to costs affect all insurers equally and so do not alter competitive positions.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement to charge all policyholders the same premium regardless of individual risk characteristics.

subsections.title: Monitoring and Reviewing Premium Rates
bullets: All items in the assumption set must be kept under review. When any assumption is no longer valid, its impact must be tested against the existing tariff before deciding whether to reprice. Changing prices incurs expense and may affect marketability.
For reviewable premium business: the actuary estimates premiums subject to periodic review. Margins are smaller because adverse experience can be corrected at the next review.
Risk of selective lapsing on rate increase: healthier lives are more likely to lapse if premiums rise significantly, leaving a worse risk pool. The rate increase must account for this risk.

In some territories, regulators specify what aspects of the basis can justify a premium review (e.g. in the UK, premiums can only increase for 'valid reason' related to general portfolio experience, not individual experience).
Where premiums are inadequate (e.g. loss leaders), reserves must be established. Their size depends on the speed of premium review and the likelihood of renewal as rates rise.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for healthier lives to lapse when premiums are increased, leaving the remaining pool with worse-than-average risk.

exam_qa.q: Why do reviewable premium products include smaller margins than guaranteed premium products?
exam_qa.a: Because any significant deterioration in future experience can be corrected at the next premium review. Guaranteed premium products cannot be repriced, so they need much larger margins to cover possible adverse experience over the full policy term.

exam_qa.q: Why must actuaries be careful not to increase reviewable premiums by too much?
exam_qa.a: A large increase encourages selective lapsing — healthier lives leave, leaving a worse risk pool. This can worsen the insurer's position, as the per-policy expense cost also rises on a smaller portfolio, potentially negating the benefit of the rate increase.

subsections.title: Nature and Terms of Health Options
bullets: Mortality/morbidity options (health options) allow policyholders to extend term or increase cover at normal premium rates without providing further medical evidence.

Common examples: option to purchase additional cover without evidence of health at normal rates; option to renew a CI policy at term end without further evidence; buy-back option to reinstate mortality cover after an accelerated CI claim.
Terms and conditions are designed to reduce anti-selection risk. Options may only be exercisable at fixed points (e.g. every 5 years) or on qualifying life events (e.g. marriage, new job).
For IP options, the policyholder must not be currently claiming or within a deferred/linked-claim period when exercising the option. Someone who has recovered may be eligible again.
Unlike life options (exercisable provided alive), health options cannot be exercised while sick — but are still valuable to those who have deteriorating health but are not yet claiming.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the policyholder to extend or increase cover at standard rates without further medical underwriting.
An option to reinstate the mortality cover under an accelerated CI policy after a CI claim has been paid, without fresh underwriting.

exam_qa.q: Why are health options valuable even if they cannot be exercised while the policyholder is sick?
exam_qa.a: There is a significant category of policyholders who are not yet sick enough to claim but have already developed conditions that increase their future risk (e.g. hypertension). These lives benefit most from exercising options at standard rates, since they would face rated or declined terms if underwritten afresh.

subsections.title: The Cost of Options
bullets: The cost of an option is the value of the excess of the premium that should be charged (given full underwriting information) over the normal premium rate that is actually charged.
The total cost depends on: (1) the health status of those who exercise the option; (2) the proportion of eligible lives who choose to exercise it.
In general, the smaller the proportion exercising the option, the worse the average health of those who do exercise it.
Cost of option ≈ proportion of lives exercising option × average extent that their health is worse than select.
If all eligible policyholders exercise the option (including healthy ones), the insurer is better off because healthy lives pay appropriate premiums, generating additional profit.
The value of options must be kept under constant review — a widespread disease outbreak could cause option costs to far exceed original pricing assumptions.

exam_qa.q: Is the insurer better off if all policyholders exercise the option, or only the unhealthy ones?
exam_qa.a: Better off if all policyholders exercise it. Healthy policyholders pay premiums appropriate to their select morbidity, generating additional profits. If only unhealthy lives exercise the option, the insurer bears the full anti-selection cost with no offsetting income from healthy lives.

subsections.title: Valuation Method and Assumptions
bullets: Options are normally valued using cashflow projections including: additional benefits expected under the option; additional premiums received (based on standard rates for a select life at the option date); any additional expenses (e.g. reminder letters, processing costs).
For pricing purposes, the valuation must also allow for additional reserves held before and after exercise.

Key assumptions required: probability that the option is exercised at each possible date; additional benefit level chosen; expected morbidity of lives who exercise vs those who do not; additional expenses.
Conventional method: assume all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum additional benefit. Simple but unrealistic; likely to overstate cost.
North American method: use more realistic take-up rate assumptions varying by exercise date and alternative option. More accurate but requires data (historically available only in North America).

Typically, the expected morbidity of lives who take up the option is heavier than those who do not (due to anti-selection). This can be expressed as a percentage loading on the base table (e.g. 150%) or an age addition (e.g. age x experiences mortality of age x+5).

A consistent set of assumptions is needed: the average mortality of all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) should remain at the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier, non-exercisers must be assumed lighter to maintain the average.
Stochastic modelling can be used to establish the core cost of options, simulating the distribution of take-up rates and subsequent morbidity across many scenarios.

key_terms.definition: Values options by assuming all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum benefit. Simple but tends to overstate cost.
Values options using realistic take-up rate assumptions based on observed experience. More accurate but requires credible data.

exam_qa.q: Why does a policy with a morbidity option need higher reserves than the equivalent policy without?
exam_qa.a: The option increases morbidity risk — an option to extend term increases the exposure period, an option to increase sum insured increases the amount at risk. Reserves must increase for expected additional claims and for the uncertainty in those claims. Before exercise, the insurer faces anti-selection risk; after exercise, it holds lives of unknown health without underwriting evidence.

exam_qa.q: Why must the morbidity assumption for non-exercisers be set lower than select if exercisers are assumed heavier?
exam_qa.a: The average morbidity across all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) must equal the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier (e.g. ultimate + 10 years), non-exercisers must be lighter than ultimate to maintain the average. Failing to do this would implicitly assume the overall portfolio is worse than expected.

subsections.title: Pricing and Reserving for Guarantees
bullets: Guaranteed premium rates must be assessed on a resilience basis: will the premium be sufficient to meet all outgoings under a range of adverse scenarios, with an agreed level of confidence?
The actuary must incorporate into the premium a loading for the additional cost of providing the guarantee, including the cost of holding guarantee reserves.
If the true cost of guarantees is reflected in premiums, it may encourage policyholders to prefer reviewable contracts (same benefits, lower premium because no guarantee cost).

Conventional (without-profits) contracts transfer mortality/morbidity, expense and investment risk from policyholder to insurer. Health and care contracts rarely include financially onerous investment guarantees because reserves are small relative to benefit amounts.
Exception: pre-funded LTCI, where large reserves accumulate and investment risk is material if the contract is written on fully guaranteed terms.
The local regulator will normally want to review the techniques and outputs used to justify the adequacy of guaranteed premiums and reserves.

key_terms.definition: Assessment of whether premium rates remain sufficient under a range of adverse scenarios, required when pricing guaranteed contracts.
An additional reserve held to cover the expected cost of providing a premium or benefit guarantee under a long-term contract.

exam_qa.q: Why do most health and care contracts not pose onerous investment guarantees on insurers?
exam_qa.a: Most health and care products are protection rather than savings contracts. Policy reserves are usually small relative to benefit amounts, so even significant underperformance of investment returns has little financial impact on the insurer. The main risk is claim incidence, not investment returns.

exam_qa.q: What is the risk of fully reflecting the guarantee cost in the premium?
exam_qa.a: It may make the guaranteed product significantly more expensive than an equivalent reviewable product. Policyholders may then opt for the reviewable contract, undermining sales volumes for the guaranteed product.

subsections.title: Reasons for Calculating Reserves
bullets: Reserves are calculated for multiple purposes: published accounts; supervisory solvency accounts; internal management accounts; estimating future premium requirements; merger/acquisition valuation; influencing investment strategy; assessing reinsurance arrangements.

The choice of assumptions depends on the purpose: published accounts use true-and-fair (best estimate or slightly prudent); supervisory accounts use the most prudent basis to protect policyholders (or best estimate with explicit risk margin); internal accounts use best estimate for a realistic view; pricing uses best estimate (possibly slightly prudent); M&A uses best estimate (optimistic for seller, prudent for purchaser); investment strategy and reinsurance assessment use best estimate.

key_terms.definition: The liabilities held by an insurer to meet its obligations to policyholders, calculated on a basis appropriate to the purpose of the valuation.

exam_qa.q: Why does the reserving basis differ depending on the purpose of the calculation?
exam_qa.a: Different purposes require different levels of prudence. Supervisory reserves protect policyholders and require the most prudent basis. Published accounts require a true-and-fair view. Internal management accounts require best estimates for realistic decision-making. M&A valuations reflect the interests of buyer vs seller. The basis should match the purpose for which results will be used.

subsections.title: Long-Term Insurance Reserves
bullets: Reserves for policies: the discounted present value of future expected claims + expenses − future premiums. The main reserve for long-term contracts, held when future outgo exceeds future income.

IBNR (Incurred But Not Reported): reserves for claims that have occurred but not yet been notified. Increasingly common in CI insurance as policyholders claim retrospectively upon discovering they had cover for a condition (often via brokers).
Reserves for reported but not yet settled claims: e.g. IP claims in payment or CI claims still in assessment.
Option reserves: additional reserves for options that may 'come into the money', e.g. guaranteed conversion options on CI policies.
Investment mismatching reserve: required in some territories to cover the extent to which assets do not match liabilities.

For a healthy IP policyholder: hold policy reserve + option reserve. If sick in deferred period (unreported): add IBNR. If reported but in deferred period: add reserve for expected cost allowing for probability of surviving through DP. If claiming: add reserve for current claim annuity value of benefits in payment.

key_terms.definition: Incurred But Not Reported — a reserve for claims that have occurred but have not yet been notified to the insurer.
An additional reserve held for the expected cost of options that policyholders may exercise, such as guaranteed conversion options on CI policies.
Alternative term for the policy reserve held for lives that are healthy and not yet claiming (prospective premium reserve).
Alternative term for the claims reserve held for lives currently receiving benefit payments.

exam_qa.q: Why is IBNR becoming increasingly common in CI insurance?
exam_qa.a: Policyholders sometimes do not realise they have cover for certain conditions (as the number of covered conditions has grown dramatically). They later discover they were covered — often through brokers — and lodge retrospective claims. Improved medical screening also means conditions are now being identified that policyholders were previously unaware of.

subsections.title: Short-Term Insurance Reserves
bullets: Short-term (annually renewable) contracts do not normally require reserves beyond the next renewal date, but must hold reserves for claims arising in the unexpired period.

Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR): the retrospective approach — the balance of premiums received in respect of the unexpired period of cover. Calculated as the proportion of the risk-related premium (excluding initial expenses) attributable to the unexpired risk period.

Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR): the prospective approach — the total amount needed to cover expected claims and expenses for the unexpired risk period. URR > UPR when premiums are inadequate. The Additional Unexpired Risk Reserve (AURR) is the excess of URR over UPR.
IBNR: also required for short-term business for claims incurred but not yet reported.
Claims in transit: claims reported but not yet assessed or recorded ('pipeline claims').
Outstanding claims reserve: claims notified but not yet fully settled.

IBNER (Incurred But Not Enough Reported): adjustment (positive or negative) to outstanding claims reserve where case estimates are believed to be inaccurate. Defined separately from outstanding claims for management information and potentially different tax treatment.
Equalisation/catastrophe reserves: amounts held back from profitable years for use in loss-making years (equalisation) or for potential future catastrophes. Regulation varies by territory.

key_terms.definition: The balance of premiums received in respect of the unexpired period of insurance. A retrospective measure of the amount of premium not yet 'earned'.
The prospective estimate of amounts needed to cover future claims and expenses for the unexpired risk period. Exceeds UPR when premiums are inadequate.
Incurred But Not Enough Reported — an adjustment to the outstanding claims reserve to reflect under- or over-estimation of case estimates.
Amounts held back in profitable years to smooth results in less profitable years. May receive favourable tax treatment depending on territory.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between UPR and URR?
exam_qa.a: UPR is retrospective — it is the proportion of premiums already received that relates to the unexpired period of cover. URR is prospective — it is the actual amount needed to cover future claims and expenses for the unexpired period. The URR exceeds UPR when premiums are inadequate, with the excess being the AURR.

exam_qa.q: Why is IBNER defined separately from the outstanding claims reserve?
exam_qa.a: It provides more information to management for better decision-making; IBNER may show different trends from other reserves, making separate modelling valuable; and IBNER may receive different tax treatment from the main outstanding claims reserve.

subsections.title: Case Estimates
bullets: Case-by-case method: an experienced claims manager inspects each claim individually and estimates the ultimate outgo. Straightforward for lump-sum claims; requires assessment of likely duration for income-based claims.

For PMI case estimates, the assessor considers: procedure type; hospital to be used; surgeon/consultant; policy coverage (excess, limits, etc.); age, gender and past claims history; current medical inflation levels.
Advantages: uses all known data; allows qualitative judgement; may be the only option when statistical models are not reliable.

Disadvantages: relies on individual skill/judgement (bias risk); extremely difficult to check; very expensive for large portfolios; inconsistent inflation assumptions; cannot estimate IBNR (unreported claims must be estimated separately).
Case estimates cannot be used for IBNR — unreported claims must be estimated using statistical methods.

key_terms.definition: An individual assessment of the expected ultimate cost of a specific outstanding claim, performed by an experienced claims manager.

exam_qa.q: When is case estimation more suitable than statistical estimation?
exam_qa.a: When there is sufficient qualitative data per claim and an experienced assessment team; when there is insufficient historical data for statistical methods; when claims are highly heterogeneous; for new classes of business; when there is high variance in claim amounts; or when no appropriate statistical model exists.

subsections.title: Statistical Estimates
bullets: Statistical estimation: appropriate for homogeneous claims where the portfolio is large enough and experience is stable. Outstanding claims are assessed in aggregate using historical trends and patterns, adjusted for known future changes.
The portfolio is segmented by contract type, distribution type or region. A statistical distribution is fitted to past experience to estimate claims from earned premium.
